planting.note: Sow in place as soon as soil is workable; dislikes transplanting.

directions: Direct sow godetia into lean, well-drained soil in early spring, barely covering the fine seed. It resents rich soil and heavy feeding, which produce foliage at the expense of the satiny cup-shaped blooms. Thin seedlings to 8-12 inches and keep evenly moist until established, then water sparingly. Cool weather intensifies the pink and lavender flower colors, so it performs best in spring and early summer before extreme heat.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in mild-winter regions.

directions: Sow this California native directly where it is to grow, as it dislikes root disturbance. Press seed lightly into cool, moist soil and expect germination within a few weeks. The low mounding plants bear sky-blue flowers with white centers and thrive in the cool weather of spring. Provide light afternoon shade in hot climates and keep soil consistently moist to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Seeds need darkness to germinate; cover completely.

directions: Cover the seed completely because Drummond phlox requires darkness to germinate. Direct sow in early spring in mild climates or start indoors six weeks before the last frost. The clustered flowers come in a wide range of colors and make excellent low bedding and cut arrangements. Deadhead regularly and shear back leggy plants in midsummer to encourage a fresh flush of bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors 6 weeks early; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start Swan River daisy indoors about six weeks before the last frost for earliest bloom. The dainty, fragrant daisies in blue, mauve, and white cover mounding plants ideal for edging, containers, and hanging baskets. Provide full sun and well-drained soil, avoiding overly rich conditions that soften growth. Shear lightly after the first flush and feed lightly to keep the plant blooming through summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall or early spring; a reliable wildflower for meadows.

directions: Scatter tidy tips seed on cool, moist soil in fall or early spring and rake in lightly. This California native produces cheerful yellow daisies tipped in white that attract native bees and butterflies. It thrives on lean soil with minimal water once established, making it ideal for wildflower meadows and drought-tolerant plantings. Allow some flowers to set seed for reliable self-sowing in future seasons.

planting.note: Direct sow in place; tolerates poor dry soil well.

directions: Direct sow this western native in early spring, barely covering the small seed. Farewell to spring produces spikes of ruffled pink to lavender flowers on slender upright stems that make good cut flowers. It prefers lean, dry soil and full sun, blooming as spring transitions into summer. Avoid overwatering and fertilizing, which reduce flowering, and let plants self-sow for future displays.

planting.note: Succession sow every few weeks for continuous bloom.

directions: Sow annual candytuft directly in early spring, making successive sowings every few weeks for a long season of bloom. The flat clusters of white, pink, and purple flowers are fragrant and attract bees and butterflies. Grow in full sun and average well-drained soil, thinning seedlings to prevent crowding. Shear plants after flowering to keep them tidy and encourage a second flush.

planting.note: Sow in place in early spring for summer color.

directions: Direct sow annual painted daisy in early spring where plants are to bloom. The colorful banded daisies with contrasting central rings make superb long-lasting cut flowers. Grow in full sun and average, well-drained soil, staking taller plants in windy sites. Deadhead frequently to prolong flowering, and the plants often self-sow lightly in favorable conditions.

planting.note: Sow directly; resents transplanting due to taproot.

directions: Direct sow annual mallow after the last frost, as it forms a taproot and resents transplanting. The large satiny hibiscus-like blooms in shades of pink and white appear all summer on bushy plants. Provide full sun and moderately fertile, well-drained soil, spacing generously for good air circulation. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and deadhead to keep the display fresh.

planting.note: Direct sow in place; short-lived, so succession sow.

directions: Broadcast scarlet flax seed thinly on prepared soil in early spring and rake in lightly. The brilliant red saucer-shaped flowers dance atop wiry stems and are a favorite in wildflower mixes. Because individual plants are short-lived, make successive sowings for continuous color through the season. It thrives in full sun and lean, well-drained soil with minimal supplemental water.

planting.note: Sow directly; a top pollinator and self-sowing native.

directions: Direct sow plains coreopsis on the soil surface, as light aids germination. This North American native bears masses of yellow daisies banded in mahogany-red that attract bees and butterflies. It tolerates poor, dry soils and full sun, making it excellent for meadows and low-maintenance borders. Allow some seed to ripen for dependable self-sowing, or deadhead to extend the bloom period.

planting.note: Start indoors 8 weeks early; surface sow for light.

directions: Surface sow this unique deep-red rudbeckia indoors eight weeks before the last frost, as light aids germination. Cherry Brandy produces rich burgundy daisies with dark centers, prized as long-lasting cut flowers. Transplant to full sun and average well-drained soil after frost danger passes. Deadhead regularly to keep new blooms coming, and treat it as an annual though it may overwinter in mild zones.

planting.note: Sow in place; needs darkness to germinate, cover seed.

directions: Sow blue lace flower directly where it is to grow, covering seed completely since it needs darkness to germinate. The powder-blue umbels resemble a colored Queen Anne's lace and are cherished by florists for airy arrangements. Provide full sun, well-drained soil, and support for the slender stems in exposed sites. Keep evenly moist during establishment and stagger sowings for a longer cutting season.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring for late spring color.

directions: Direct sow viscaria thinly in early spring where plants are to bloom. The dainty rose, blue, and white five-petaled flowers cover slender stems and suit cottage gardens and cutting beds. It appreciates full sun and well-drained soil and blooms quickly from seed. Thin seedlings for good air movement and deadhead to prolong the flowering period.

planting.note: Surface sow on cool soil; do not cover the fine seed.

directions: Scatter the tiny Shirley poppy seed on the surface of cool, moist soil in very early spring, or sow in fall in mild regions. Do not cover the seed, which needs light and cold to germinate well. The delicate crepe-paper flowers in pink, red, and white are classic cottage and meadow plants. Poppies resent transplanting, so always direct sow, and let some pods ripen for self-seeding.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; thrives in heat.

directions: Direct sow tassel flower after the last frost in warm, well-drained soil. The small brush-like scarlet and orange flowers rise on wiry stems above tidy rosettes, adding fine texture to arrangements. It thrives in heat and full sun and blooms freely all summer with little care. Deadhead to prolong flowering and allow occasional self-sowing for future plants.

planting.note: Surface sow; compact form good for edging.

directions: Surface sow dwarf calliopsis where it is to grow, pressing seed into contact with the soil since light aids germination. This compact selection forms neat mounds smothered in bicolor yellow-and-red daisies loved by pollinators. It tolerates poor, dry soil and full sun and is ideal for edging, containers, and the front of borders. Deadhead or shear lightly to encourage continued bloom into fall.

planting.note: Surface sow; needs light and dislikes transplanting.

directions: Direct sow mignonette on the soil surface, as it needs light to germinate and resents root disturbance. The unremarkable greenish flower spikes release an intensely sweet raspberry-like fragrance beloved in Victorian gardens. Grow in full sun to light shade in cool weather and fertile, alkaline soil. Position it near paths and seating where its perfume can be enjoyed, and make successive sowings for a long season.

planting.note: Direct sow; drought tolerant and heat loving.

directions: Direct sow star of the veldt in warm, well-drained soil after frost danger passes. This South African annual bears glowing orange and apricot daisies that open in bright sun and close on cloudy days. It thrives in heat and lean soil with minimal water, making it excellent for dry sunny beds and containers. Deadhead spent blooms and avoid overwatering, which can cause rot.

planting.note: Start indoors 6 weeks early for cut flowers.

directions: Start sweet scabious indoors about six weeks before the last frost, or direct sow once soil warms. The domed pincushion flowers in deep maroon, pink, and white are long-lasting cut flowers and pollinator magnets. Grow in full sun and fertile, well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil, staking tall stems if needed. Harvest or deadhead frequently to keep the plants producing new blooms all summer.

planting.note: Start indoors 8 weeks early; sow seed on edge.

directions: Start cup and saucer vine indoors eight weeks before the last frost, sowing the flat seeds on edge to prevent rot. This vigorous climber uses tendrils to scale trellises and can reach twenty feet in a single season. The large bell-shaped flowers open greenish and mature to deep purple, resembling a cup on a leafy saucer. Transplant after frost into full sun and rich, moist soil, providing sturdy support.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; climbs by twining petioles.

directions: Direct sow canary creeper after the last frost or start indoors a few weeks early in short-season areas. This relative of nasturtium climbs by twining leaf stalks and bears fringed, bright yellow flowers resembling tiny canaries. Provide a trellis or netting in full sun to partial shade with moist, moderately fertile soil. Keep well watered in summer heat and it will bloom until frost.

planting.note: Start indoors 6-8 weeks early for earlier bloom.

directions: Start black-eyed Susan vine indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost for the longest season of bloom. The twining stems carry cheerful orange, yellow, or white flowers each with a dark central eye. Provide a trellis, fence, or container support in full sun to partial shade with rich, evenly moist soil. Feed lightly and keep well watered, and the vine will flower nonstop until frost.

planting.note: Soak and nick seed; start indoors 6 weeks early.

directions: Nick and soak the hard seeds overnight, then start Spanish flag indoors six weeks before the last frost. The vigorous vine produces one-sided sprays of tubular flowers that shade from scarlet through orange to creamy yellow. Provide a strong trellis in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil, transplanting after all frost danger passes. Hummingbirds and butterflies flock to the blooms, which continue until first frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after soil warms; loves heat.

directions: Direct sow hyacinth bean after the soil has warmed, or start indoors a few weeks early in cool climates. This ornamental vine bears purple stems, spikes of pea-like lavender flowers, and glossy magenta seed pods. Provide a sturdy trellis in full sun and average soil, and it will climb rapidly in summer heat. The pods are ornamental but require thorough cooking, so it is grown chiefly for its striking display.

planting.note: Start indoors 10 weeks early; slow to establish.

directions: Start purple bell vine indoors ten weeks before the last frost, surface sowing the fine seed and providing warmth. This delicate climber produces dangling flowers with rosy-pink parasol-shaped calyces and dark tubular petals. Grow on a light trellis or in hanging baskets in full sun to partial shade with rich, moist soil. It is grown as a tender annual in most climates and blooms until frost.

planting.note: Nick seed and direct sow after frost.

directions: Nick the seed coat and direct sow firecracker vine after the last frost in warm soil. This twining morning glory relative bears small scarlet trumpet flowers with yellow throats that draw hummingbirds. Provide a fence or trellis in full sun and average soil, and it will climb quickly through summer. Deadheading is unnecessary, but remove faded flowers if you wish to limit self-seeding.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 6-8 inches deep before ground freezes.

directions: Plant Triumph tulip bulbs in fall about six to eight inches deep and four to six inches apart in well-drained soil. This mid-season class offers sturdy stems and classic egg-shaped blooms in a vast color range, excellent for beds and cutting. Provide full sun and allow the foliage to yellow naturally after bloom to recharge the bulb. In warm climates pre-chill bulbs for ten to fourteen weeks before planting, or treat them as annuals.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sheltered spot; blooms are heavy.

directions: Plant parrot tulip bulbs in autumn six to eight inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and a spot sheltered from wind. The flamboyant fringed and twisted petals are streaked and feathered in bold colors, making dramatic cut flowers. Because the large blooms can flop, choose a protected location and stake if necessary. Let foliage die back naturally, and in mild-winter areas chill bulbs before planting.

planting.note: Plant in fall; perennializes well in warm dry climates.

directions: Plant lady tulip bulbs in fall about five inches deep in sharply drained soil and full sun. Unlike hybrid tulips, this dainty species perennializes reliably and naturalizes in warm, dry climates. The slender flowers are white or yellow with a bold red or pink exterior stripe and open flat in sun. Keep the site dry during summer dormancy to encourage the bulbs to return and multiply for years.

planting.note: Plant in fall; fragrant and good for warm climates.

directions: Plant jonquilla daffodil bulbs in fall about six inches deep in well-drained soil and full to partial sun. These fragrant narcissi carry several small golden flowers per stem and tolerate warmer climates better than most daffodils. They naturalize freely and are resistant to deer and rodents. Allow the grassy foliage to ripen fully before removal to build strong bulbs for next year.

planting.note: Plant in fall; includes paperwhites for forcing.

directions: Plant tazetta daffodil bulbs in fall six inches deep in well-drained soil, or force paperwhite types indoors in pebbles and water. These clustered, intensely fragrant narcissi are well suited to mild-winter gardens and bloom early. Give them full sun and let the foliage die back naturally outdoors. Forced bulbs are generally discarded after flowering, while garden bulbs naturalize in suitable zones.

planting.note: Plant in fall; wear gloves as bulbs can irritate skin.

directions: Plant multiflora hyacinth bulbs in fall about six inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun. Unlike single-spike hyacinths, this type sends up several looser, fragrant flower stalks per bulb for a naturalistic effect. Wear gloves when handling, as the bulbs can irritate sensitive skin. Deadhead after bloom and let foliage ripen; flower size may diminish over the years as it perennializes.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall; naturalizes and resists squirrels.

directions: Plant snow crocus corms in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil and full to partial sun. This early species crocus naturalizes rapidly in lawns and borders and is more squirrel-resistant than large Dutch crocus. The slender lavender flowers are among the first blooms of late winter, providing vital early forage for bees. Leave the foliage undisturbed until it withers to allow the corms to multiply.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in drifts for best effect.

directions: Plant Dutch crocus corms in fall three to four inches deep in well-drained soil, setting them in generous drifts. The large goblet-shaped flowers in purple, white, and yellow open in early spring for bees emerging from winter. Grow in full sun to light shade and avoid summer irrigation while corms are dormant. Let the grassy foliage ripen fully, and the corms will multiply into showy colonies over time.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost; lift and store where not hardy.

directions: Plant dinnerplate dahlia tubers after the last frost, laying them horizontally about six inches deep with the eye facing up. These giant blooms can reach ten to twelve inches across and require staking at planting to support the tall stems. Grow in full sun and rich, well-drained soil, watering deeply once shoots emerge and pinching to promote branching. In cold climates lift and store the tubers in a frost-free place over winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost; excellent long-lasting cut flower.

directions: Plant pompon dahlia tubers after the last frost about four to six inches deep in rich, well-drained soil. The small, perfectly spherical, tightly quilled flowers are outstanding long-lasting cut flowers. Grow in full sun, stake the stems, and pinch young plants to encourage more blooms. Deadhead regularly and, in cold zones, dig and store the tubers over winter after the first frost blackens the foliage.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring; never let dry out.

directions: Plant trumpet lily bulbs in fall or early spring about eight inches deep in deep, fertile, well-drained soil. The tall stems carry many large, powerfully fragrant trumpet-shaped flowers in midsummer and often need staking. Give them full sun with cool, shaded roots and never allow the bulbs to dry out completely. Leave stems standing until they yellow so the bulbs can store energy for the following year.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall; propagates from stem bulbils.

directions: Plant tiger lily bulbs in fall six to eight inches deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. The recurved orange flowers heavily spotted with black nod in mid to late summer atop tall sturdy stems. This vigorous heirloom produces small dark bulbils in the leaf axils that can be planted to grow new bulbs. It is a known carrier of viruses, so plant it well away from other prized lilies.

planting.note: Plant pips in fall or early spring in shade.

directions: Plant lily of the valley pips in fall or early spring, setting the rhizomes just below the soil surface in humus-rich, moist shade. The arching stems of nodding white bells are intensely fragrant and spread by rhizomes to form a dense groundcover. It thrives under trees and in woodland gardens and can become invasive, so site it where spread is welcome. All parts are toxic if eaten, so keep it away from children and pets.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall; naturalizes and self-sows.

directions: Plant drumstick allium bulbs in fall about four inches deep in well-drained soil and full sun. The egg-shaped flower heads emerge green and mature to deep burgundy on wiry stems in early summer, weaving beautifully through borders. Bees and butterflies love the blooms, and the seed heads dry well for arrangements. Keep the site on the dry side in summer and the bulbs will naturalize and self-sow over time.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall; huge seed heads dry beautifully.

directions: Plant star of Persia bulbs in fall about six inches deep in sharply drained soil and full sun. Each stem bears an enormous globe up to ten inches across of metallic star-shaped violet florets in late spring. The dried seed heads persist for weeks and are prized for arrangements. Site it in a hot, dry spot and let the bulbs bake during summer dormancy to ensure reliable return.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall; naturalizes readily in shade.

directions: Plant Spanish bluebell bulbs in fall about four inches deep in moist, well-drained woodland soil in partial shade. The upright stems carry many bell-shaped blue, pink, or white flowers in mid to late spring. It naturalizes vigorously beneath trees and shrubs and can spread aggressively, so site it thoughtfully. Allow the foliage to yellow and die back naturally to feed the bulbs for next season.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs in fall; naturalizes in lawns.

directions: Plant Siberian squill bulbs in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil in sun to partial shade. The nodding, intensely blue star-shaped flowers appear very early in spring and provide crucial forage for early bees. It naturalizes freely in lawns and under deciduous trees, forming sheets of blue over the years. Plant in generous drifts and leave the foliage to ripen so the colony can expand.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall; needs a dry summer baking.

directions: Plant dwarf reticulated iris bulbs in fall about four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun. The jewel-toned blue and purple flowers with contrasting markings bloom in late winter before most other bulbs. It needs a warm, dry summer dormancy to bloom well in subsequent years, so avoid irrigated beds. Plant it at the front of borders or in rock gardens where its early flowers can be admired up close.

planting.note: Plant large bulbs in fall for dramatic late-spring globes.

directions: Plant Globemaster allium bulbs in fall six to eight inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun. Each stout stem produces a massive violet globe up to ten inches across that lasts for weeks and draws countless bees. Position the plants among perennials that hide the fading foliage, which yellows before the flowers finish. The dried seed heads make striking architectural accents in the garden or in arrangements.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in fall in humus-rich shade.

directions: Plant wood anemone rhizomes in fall just below the surface in cool, humus-rich woodland soil in partial to full shade. The starry white spring flowers carpet the ground before deciduous trees leaf out, then the plant goes dormant by summer. It spreads slowly by creeping rhizomes to form a lovely ephemeral groundcover. Keep the soil moist during growth and mark the location, as the foliage disappears entirely in summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall promptly; they dry out quickly.

directions: Plant checkered fritillary bulbs in fall as soon as possible, since the delicate bulbs dry out quickly, setting them about four inches deep. The nodding bell flowers display a distinctive checkerboard pattern in purple or white in mid-spring. It prefers moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade and naturalizes well in damp meadows and woodland edges. Handle the fragile bulbs gently at planting and let foliage die back to ensure return.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall; tolerates wet soils.

directions: Plant summer snowflake bulbs in fall about four inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil in sun to partial shade. Despite its name it blooms in mid to late spring, bearing nodding white bells tipped with green on arching stems. Unusually among bulbs it tolerates heavy, wet soils and naturalizes along ponds and in damp borders. Leave the strappy foliage to ripen fully, and the clumps will slowly increase over the years.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall; foliage smells of onion when crushed.

directions: Plant spring starflower bulbs in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. The fragrant, star-shaped, pale blue flowers appear over grassy foliage that smells faintly of onion when bruised. It naturalizes readily in mild climates, forming spreading clumps in rock gardens and borders. Keep the site dry during summer dormancy and divide crowded clumps every few years.

planting.note: Plant tubers shallowly in late summer under trees.

directions: Plant hardy cyclamen tubers shallowly in late summer or early fall in well-drained, humus-rich soil under deciduous trees. The dainty pink or white reflexed flowers appear in autumn, followed by beautifully marbled ivy-shaped leaves that persist through winter. It thrives in dry shade where little else grows and slowly self-sows to form colonies. Keep the tubers on the dry side during their summer dormancy and mulch lightly with leaf mold.

planting.note: Plant tuberous roots in spring; superb cut flower.

directions: Plant Peruvian lily tuberous roots in spring about six inches deep in rich, well-drained soil, handling the brittle roots carefully. The orchid-like flowers in warm oranges, pinks, and reds are among the longest-lasting of all cut flowers. Grow in full sun to light shade with consistent moisture, and pull rather than cut spent stems to stimulate rebloom. In colder zones mulch heavily for winter or lift the roots for storage.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after soil warms; lift where not hardy.

directions: Plant pineapple lily bulbs in spring about five inches deep in rich, well-drained soil once the ground has warmed. Each stout stem produces a dense spike of starry flowers topped by a tuft of leafy bracts resembling a pineapple. Grow in full sun with regular summer moisture, and it also performs beautifully in large containers. In cold climates lift the bulbs before frost or grow them in pots moved indoors for winter.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring; naturalizes and can spread widely.

directions: Plant montbretia corms in spring three to four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. The arching sprays of fiery orange to red tubular flowers in mid to late summer are magnets for hummingbirds. It multiplies rapidly by cormlets and can spread widely, so site it where vigorous growth is welcome. Divide congested clumps every few years and mulch for winter in the colder end of its range.

planting.note: Soak tubers overnight before planting in fall.

directions: Soak the small knobby tubers overnight, then plant Grecian windflower in fall about two to three inches deep in well-drained, humus-rich soil. The daisy-like flowers in blue, pink, or white carpet the ground in early spring beneath deciduous trees and shrubs. It naturalizes freely in sun to partial shade and goes dormant by early summer. Plant in generous drifts and leave the foliage to ripen so the colony can expand year after year.
